2.2 Project Description 
The project aim was to assess the energy generation and thermal efficiency performance of a 
TEG system and its potential as a solution for waste-heat-to-power generation in industrial 
applications, primarily cement plants. 
 
A significant amount of industrial energy input is lost as waste heat. TEGs can be used to 
reclaim part of the waste heat and convert it to electricity, offsetting a portion of the energy use. 
Historically, TEGs have been used primarily in small and remote applications and have had a 
thermal efficiency of 2%–5% (DOE 2008). Prior industrial-scale TEG systems, such as a unit 
offered by Alphabet Energy, operated at an efficiency of 2.5% (Lamonica 2014). 
 
ATS has installed a small-scale TEG system at a Holcim plant in Alpena, Michigan, to 
demonstrate its ability. The system is pilot-scale to showcase the technology feasibility and 
proof of concept for commercial-scale systems. The pilot was evaluated against ATS’ claims of 
system performance. These results were used to estimate potential annual impacts of a future 
commercial-scale system. 
 
The ATS TEG system design consists of a thermoelectric array comprising individual 
thermoelectric semiconductor cartridges, each of which converts waste heat to electricity, 
contributing to the overall power generation of the system. According to ATS, the TEG pilot 
system is expected to operate with a total average thermal efficiency of approximately 4.8% at 
Holcim’s cement plant.  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   23 
2.3 Thermoelectric Generator Technology Description 
By leveraging the Seebeck effect, the ATS TEG system generates electricity from the voltage 
created when a temperature differential is applied across two different semiconductors 
(ScienceDirect n.d.). The voltages created are proportional to the magnitude of the temperature 
differential of the hot and cold side of the cartridge, and thus, maintaining large temperature 
differentials is desired to maximize generation capabilities. The main disadvantage of TEG 
systems is the low efficiency in converting thermal heat to electricity, only ranging 2%–5% 
relative to other heat recovery systems. However, TEG’s direct approach requires minimal 
moving parts, offering reliable, lower maintenance, and quiet operation compared to traditional 
fluid-based heat recovery systems. ATS’ modular TEG design can be integrated into existing 
infrastructure, potentially providing energy and associated cost savings. 
 
Figure 3 illustrates the TEG’s modular power generation array showing various components, 
including pellets, cartridges, strings of cartridges, and inverters. Each pellet is capable of 
generating 20 millivolts and 4.6 amperes from the temperature differential applied to the 
system; 276 pellets are connected in series to construct a cartridge. The cartridges are then 
arranged in series, with eight cartridges forming a string designed to deliver 680 watts (W) of 
power. The system features 19 inverters connected in parallel, each supporting four parallel 
strings of cartridges. The inverters are then connected to the busbar to connect to the 
installation site. This design is modular, allowing scalability and adaptability to various industrial 
applications.  
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   24 
 
 
Figure 3. Schematic showing the hierarchy of a TEG cartridge 
 
The TEG pilot-scale unit, housed in a shipping container 20 feet long, 8 feet wide, and 8.5 feet 
tall, consists of an array of 582 total individual TEG cartridges sandwiched between 48 hot 
plates and 49 cold plates. Of the 582 cartridges, 573 were active for the test. The hot plates 
were designed to operate at 240°C for maximum efficiency with a physical limit of 250°C, above 
which the cartridge solder material would melt. The cartridges have a design wattage of 20 W 
per cartridge, and thus the 573 cartridge system has an anticipated gross power capacity of 
11.5 kilowatts (kW). The system uses inverters that activate at a hot plate temperature of 150°C 
to convert the electricity generated from the temperature difference into usable power. The ATS 
cartridges are 94.2 mm by 162 mm (3.7 inches x 6.4 inches) per cartridge.  
 
For the evaluation, a portion of the steam generated from the waste heat of the kilns at Holcim 
Alpena was diverted from the existing steam turbine generation system for use as the TEG 
heat source. A heat exchanger extracted the waste heat into a hot thermal oil, which was 
pumped to the hot plates to provide the heat necessary for operation (150°–240°C). The TEG 
cold plates were serviced by water from Lake Huron.  
 
The parasitic load is the power drawn to operate the TEG system that includes hot and cold 
loop pumps, programmable logic controller, and other supporting equipment and devices. 
Electricity is generated at the cartridges, then transmitted to the main panel board via inverters. 
Figure 4 shows a 3D rendering of the TEG pilot-scale system. Figure 5 shows a photo of the 
cartridge array of the TEG pilot system.

ATS designed the TEG system to be modular and scalable to meet the waste heat profile for a 
wide variety of industries and applications. Based on discussions with ATS staff and reviewing 
conceptual drawings, the commercial-scale unit will comprise TEG arrays (each array similar to 
the TEG pilot-scale unit) configured to maximize the waste heat profile of a given application. 
The system will include common headers to supply hot working fluid and cold-side fluid to 
linked TEG arrays. Theoretically, the number of arrays can be tailored to a specific application 
and will ultimately depend upon the gross power per cartridge of the ATS cartridges and the 
amount of waste heat available. The commercial-scale TEG system is currently under 
development with ATS and Holcim, which will be ATS’ first customer, for a system 
approximately 500 kW in capacity. For larger-scale projects, the ATS commercial system being 
designed to consist of an arrangement of 1-megawatt TEG units, each of which will fit in a 
standard shipping container, with additional storage required for supplementary equipment. 
 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   27 
 
3. Technology Demonstration Intent 
 
The objective of this evaluation is to assess the effectiveness of a small-scale TEG system’s 
ability to capture waste heat energy from a kiln and convert it into electricity.  
 
The key performance indicators assessed as part of the evaluation process were: 
 
• Array Performance: An assessment of the performance of the total pilot-scale unit 
consisting of an array of 573 active TEG cartridges. 
 
o Gross power (kW): The measure of the total power generated by the TEG pilot-
scale unit. 
 
o Net power (kW): The actual usable power delivered after subtracting the 
parasitic power (the power required for TEG loads such as pumps, 
programmable logic controllers, etc.) from the gross power generated by the 
system.  
 
o Thermal efficiency: The measure of the TEG’s ability to convert waste heat 
entering the system into usable, gross power. This is represented as the 
percentage of heat entering the system converted into power. 
 
• Cartridge Performance: An assessment of individual TEG cartridges to capture 
performance variability throughout the array and identify opportunities for future 
optimization.  
 
o Cartridge Gross Power (W): The measure of power generated by each 
individual thermoelectric semiconductor cartridge of the pilot-scale system. 
 
o Cartridge Thermal Efficiency (%): The measure of each cartridge’s ability to 
convert the heat provided into usable gross power.  
 
Additionally, the study sought to determine the potential annual impacts of scaling the pilot 
system to a commercial-scale system for annual operation at Holcim Alpena and for a more 
typical suspension preheater and pre-calciner kilns. The evaluation estimated gross and net 
annual electric generation that could be achieved by capturing heat from the two proposed tap-
in points: the kiln exhaust and the clinker cooler exhaust.7  
 
7 ATS proposed three potential tap-in points: convective heat capture from the kiln exhaust and the clinker 
cooler exhaust, and radiant heat capture from the kiln shell. Note that this evaluation focuses on the 
convective heat capture points only, due to the data availability and complexity of analysis that would be 
required to estimate heat capture from a radiant heat source.  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   28 
 
As discussed, unlike other plants, Alpena is unique in using waste heat from the kilns to 
generate electricity through microturbines. However, most of Holcim’s other cement plants, as 
well as those operated by others, currently lack a solution for harnessing lower temperature 
waste heat. Holcim’s overall objective is to assess the potential for waste heat recovery using 
ATS’ TEG, assuming available waste heat can be harnessed. Data collected from the Alpena 
plant, including steam parameters, clinker production, feed rate, stack inlet temperature, and 
flue gas flow, will be used to determine broader application of the technology at Holcim cement 
facilities in the United States. Additionally, the close proximity of Lake Huron, with an average 
surface temperature of 0.8°–20.4°C (NOAA n.d.-a), to the Alpena site presents a unique 
opportunity to use the lake water directly in the cold loop of the TEG. This may not be available 
at other plants, requiring the installation of a cooling loop. 
 
The following two applications were examined to assess the impact as part of this study:  
 
• Holcim Alpena: The Holcim Alpena site consists of long dry kilns with superheater 
boilers, which differs from the rest of Holcim’s cement plant portfolio and results in 
lower-grade waste heat. The study estimates gross and net annual generation using the 
superheater boiler exhaust and clinker cooler exhaust, based on 2023 operational data.  
 
• Typical Installation at Cement Plants: Common cement plants have preheater kilns 
with higher exhaust temperatures than Holcim Alpena across a range of production 
rates. The study estimates gross and net annual generation using the preheater 
exhaust and clinker cooler exhaust, with a sensitivity analysis to account for the typical 
range of preheater exhaust temperatures, clinker cooler exhaust temperatures, and 
clinker production rates.  
 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   29 
4. Evaluation Scope and Boundary 
4.1 IPMVP Option 
The evaluation methodology followed the International Performance Measurement and 
Verification Protocol (IPMVP), developed by the Efficiency Valuation Organization (EVO n.d.).  
The IPMVP outlines four options depending on the purpose, scope, and objective of the project. 
These four options are categorized into two general types: retrofit isolation and whole facility. 
Retrofit-isolation methods consider only the affected equipment or system independent of the 
rest of the facility. Whole-facility methods consider the total building or facility energy use and 
de-emphasize specific equipment performance. The primary difference in these approaches is 
where the measurement boundary is drawn. Options A and B are retrofit-isolation methods, 
Option C is a whole-facility method, and Option D can be used as either but is usually applied 
as a whole-facility method. 
 
This measurement and verification (M&V) approach followed “IPMVP Option B – Retrofit 
Isolation, All Parameters Measurement” with the necessary adjustments to account for the 
variations in operating conditions. For the TEG pilot-scale unit, gross power, parasitic power, 
and all operating parameters were logged during the test period. The TEG system was first 
installed in December 2022 and was operated for the evaluation from March 9, 2024 to March 
11, 2024.  
 
4.2 Measurement Boundary Descriptions 
The system boundary includes the steam-to-working-fluid heat exchanger (HX-001), the 
cartridge array (HX-005), the expansion tank and pumps of the hot-side and the cold-side 
loops, and additional supplementary equipment including the programmable logic controller and 
power equipment.  
 
Figure 6 provides a control screen that shows the measurement boundary of the evaluation for 
the TEG pilot-scale unit.  
 
4.3 Evaluation Setup 
The evaluation setup is shown in  
 
Figure 6. The setup consisted of the 582 installed cartridge (573 operating) ATS TEG array 
(HX-005) that uses the temperature differential between a working fluid—DOWTHERM Q 
thermal oil (See Appendix Section C.vii for fluid characteristics)—and a cold-side fluid—water 
from Lake Huron—to generate power. Heat from the plant was captured by a heat exchanger 
(HX-001) that converted heat from waste steam to heat in the array working fluid. Steam at 
Holcim is generated from kiln waste heat and supplied to turbine generators. A heat source was 
provided by tapping into an existing steam line and diverting a portion of the steam to the pilot-
scale unit in order to evaluate the TEG system. The working-fluid thermal oil was fed through 
an expansion tank and two pumps to move the fluid through the TEG array and the HX-001 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   30 
heat exchanger. The cold side of the array was supplied by water from Lake Huron. Though 
cold-side fluid pumps were installed, the lake water pressure at the plant was sufficient to 
supply water to the TEG array; thus, the pumps were not used. 
 
 
 
Figure 6. Control screen showing the boundary of the evaluation for the TEG pilot-scale system8 
 
To assess the range of TEG performance, the system was evaluated at various operating 
conditions. Based on data provided by the site for available waste heat and lake temperatures, 
the TEG was tested at three target hot-loop plate-face inlet temperatures (160°C, 200°C, and 
240°C) and three target cold-loop plate-face exit temperatures (5°–6°C, 25°C, and 30°C) (Table 
2Error! Reference source not found.). The target test temperature of 240°C on the hot-side 
loop was not fully achieved due to varying plant conditions and insufficient heat capacity from 
the incoming steam. The cold-side loop temperature was limited by environmental conditions, 
since the water is sourced from Lake Huron, and the 5°–6°C cold-side loop inlet temperature is 
considered “best effort” based on the available conditions during the test. The target exit 
temperatures of the cold-loop plate faces were achieved by adjusting the flow rate of the cold-
side fluid. 
 
The TEG system was operated for each of the combinations (a total of nine tests) under 
steady-state conditions for at least two hours across three days, March 9–11, 2024. Table 2 
shows the targeted testing conditions for each day of the test period to evaluate the 
performance of the TEG system.

5.2 Data Cleaning 
Data cleaning is a crucial step in the data preparation process and the foundation for the 
subsequent data analysis. It involves identifying and rectifying errors or inconsistencies in 
datasets to ensure the data is accurate, reliable, and suitable for analysis. Cleaning was 
necessary when datasets were incomplete or included outlier data. 
 
ATS TEG Test Data 
 
The TEG test data used for developing the predictive models was scrubbed to include only the 
data when the respective equipment was operational. All incomplete datapoints were removed. 
The system’s inverters only turn on when the hot-loop inlet temperature exceeds 150°C; 
temperatures below this threshold do not produce enough voltage to activate the inverters. As a 
result, all intervals with hot-loop inlet temperatures below 150°C were excluded from the 
analysis dataset. Three data intervals were excluded as outliers, as the calculated efficiency for 
HX-001 (heat exchanger that interfaces with the plant) exceeded 100%, possibly due to a 
sensor error: 
 
• 3/9/24 13:26 
• 3/10/24 18:12 
• 3/10/24 18:15 
 
ATS TEG Cartridge Data 
 
Cartridge data used for the analysis was filtered to only include points during which the TEG 
was operational. Any intervals showing a negative or zero power reading are indicative of 
intervals with the sensors off and were removed. The data was filtered on the upper bound at 
three standard deviations from the mean power generation for each day of testing.  
 
Holcim Alpena Kiln Data 
 
To ensure that only valid and representative data was used in the estimates of annual 
generation potential of the TEG system, the kiln operational data was examined for outliers, 
which were then excluded from the analysis. Outliers were identified as points in which the 
hourly value was greater or less than the mean plus or minus three standard deviations. Hours 
where a given kiln was off were not included in the assessment of outliers (i.e., not included in 
the calculation of the mean or the standard deviation).  
 
The kiln operational data included several missing data points for each of the trended 
parameters, appearing to result from sensor or communication failures within Holcim’s control 
system. For kiln clinker production data and kiln stack flow data, hours with missing values 
were excluded from the analysis. A significant amount of missing data was observed for the kiln 
cooler vent temperature, particularly the cooler vent temperatures for Kiln 22 and Kiln 23, which 
were missing 6,161 and 6,152 values out of 8,760 data points, respectively. For this study, the 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   36 
cooler vent temperatures from each kiln were used to calculate the weighted average waste 
heat air temperature that could theoretically be supplied to a commercial TEG system by 
combining the kiln exhaust (at the boiler) and cooler vent exhaust from all five kilns. Since data 
was still available for the remaining three kilns, the evaluation team was able to rely on data 
from those kilns during the periods when data was missing for Kiln 22 and Kiln 23 to calculate 
the hourly weighted average waste heat air temperature used to estimate annual generation 
potential of a theoretical commercial-scale system.  
 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   37 
6. Calculation Methodology 
 
The evaluation methodology consists of two parts and follows IPMVP Option B: retrofit isolation 
with all parameter measurement.  
 
The first part focused on evaluating the performance claims by assessing the generation 
performance of the ATS TEG pilot-scale unit achieved during the testing period. The collected 
field evaluation data was analyzed to determine the tested performance in terms of:  
 
• Array Performance – An assessment of the performance of the total pilot-scale unit 
consisting of an array of 582 installed (573 operating) TEG cartridges. 
 
1. Gross Power (kW) 
 
2. Net Power (kW) 
 
3. Thermal Efficiency (%) 
 
• Cartridge Performance – An assessment of the performance of individual TEG 
cartridges within the pilot-scale unit array.  
 
4. Cartridge Gross Power (W/cartridge) 
 
5. Cartridge Thermal Efficiency (%) 
 
The second part focused on estimating the annual impact of the theoretical installation of a 
commercial-scale TEG system at a cement plant. This consisted of analyzing data collected 
from the TEG pilot-scale unit field test to construct predictive models: first for gross power to 
estimate total power generation potential, and second for parasitic power to be used in the 
calculation of net power generation (i.e., the total usable power that would be provided to the 
plant). The regression models were then applied to the annual operations of the Holcim Alpena 
plant to estimate annual generation potential at the site. Using the Alpena plant production 
profile as a guide, the regression models were then applied to typical kiln operational 
parameters to develop a sensitivity analysis that demonstrates the potential range of annual 
generation potential from typical TEG commercial-scale installations at cement plants. To do 
so, the evaluation team used the analysis model created from the Holcim Alpena operational 
data, adjusted to represent typical ranges for plant operations. 
 
Both parts of the analysis rely on fundamental heat exchanger concepts and methods that will 
be referred to throughout the report. For reference, an overview of the basic heat exchanger 
concepts used is provided in Appendix Section C.i. 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   38 
6.1 Thermoelectric Generation Performance 
 
i. Array Performance Evaluation Methodology 
 
The first part of the study consisted of evaluation of the performance of the ATS TEG pilot-
scale unit. Evaluation of ATS’ TEG pilot-scale unit followed IPMVP Option B approach where all 
parameters that influence the generation of the TEG system were measured during the test 
periods, and an analysis was conducted to verify actual performance against the stated claims. 
 
Gross Power 
 
The TEG gross power for the entire array was measured directly for each interval using a 
Schneider PowerLogic PM8000 power meter. See Appendix Section A for data collection 
equipment specifications. Values for average gross power and peak gross power were 
determined across the testing period for all intervals during which the TEG system was active 
and the inverters were on (i.e., when hot-loop inlet temperatures to the TEG array were greater 
than or equal to 150°C, the activation temperature of the inverters). 
 
Net Power 
 
The net power produced by the TEG system is the difference between the gross power and the 
parasitic power, which was calculated for each interval of the test period as follows:  
 
𝑁𝑒𝑡 𝑃𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟 (𝑘𝑊) =  𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝑃𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟 (𝑘𝑊) −  𝑃𝑎𝑟𝑎𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑐 𝑃𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟 (𝑘𝑊) (1) 
 
Similar to the gross power, the TEG parasitic power used in the calculation was measured 
directly for each interval using a Schneider PowerLogic PM8000 power meter. The net power 
was then calculated using Equation 1.  
 
Values for average net power and peak net power were then determined across the testing 
period for all intervals during which the TEG system was active and the inverters were on. 
 
Thermal Efficiency  
 
The thermal efficiency of the system is the measure of the TEG’s ability to convert waste heat 
entering the system into usable, gross power. This metric is represented as the percent of heat 
entering the system converted into electrical power. For this analysis, this was calculated using 
the gross power produced and the calculated heat passing through the system in the following 
equation:  
𝑇ℎ𝑒𝑟𝑚𝑎𝑙 𝐸𝑓𝑓𝑖𝑐𝑖𝑒𝑛𝑐𝑦 (%) = 𝐺𝑟𝑜𝑠𝑠 𝑃𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟
𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝐻𝑒𝑎𝑡 𝑇𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑓𝑒𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑑 (2) 
 
The total heat transferred by the TEG system can be calculated through two methods: first at 
the array level (i.e., the total TEG system of 573 active cartridges), from the measured heat 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   39 
transferred in the working fluid entering and leaving the TEG array; or second at the cartridge 
level, from the average heat transferred within the cartridges that make up the TEG array. The 
second method sets the boundary at the cartridge level by examining the heat transferred 
entering and leaving the cartridges that comprise the TEG array. This method negates heat 
losses and thermal bridging potential that may or may not be unique to the pilot-scale unit and 
not applicable to future designs. However, the cartridge level method also relies on 
assumptions to be made for the thermal conductivity of the cartridges, expected to range from 
1.265 watts per meter-Kelvin (W/m-K) to 1.44 W/m-K, according to previous testing by ATS. 
For this analysis, both methods were examined, with the difference in the results found to be 
negligible, and both were used for different applications, as discussed in Section 7.2.ii.  
 
For the first method, total heat transferred to the array is calculated with the following 
equation:10 
 
𝑄𝐻𝑋005 = ṁℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ 𝑐𝑝ℎ−𝐻𝑋005@𝑇ℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ (𝑇ℎ𝑜𝑢𝑡−𝐻𝑋005 − 𝑇ℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005) ∗ ( 1 𝑘𝑊
3600 𝑘𝐽/ℎ) (3) 
 
 
Where: 
ṁℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 = 𝑣̇ℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ ⍴ℎ−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ (60 𝑚𝑖𝑛 ℎ⁄ ) (4) 
 
 
And: 
 
ṁhin-HX005 = the mass flow rate of the working fluid at the array inlet (kg/h)

ii. Cartridge Performance Evaluation Methodology 
 
Data was captured for each of the 573 active cartridges in the TEG array to allow assessment 
of individual cartridge performance, an important metric for understanding technology abilities 
and limitations. This data included instantaneous power draw and cartridge position.  
 
The evaluation consisted of examining the data to assess cartridge-level performance for: 
 
• Mean and Peak Gross Power (W/cartridge) – The gross power generated on an 
individual cartridge basis. 
 
• Cartridge Performance Distribution – An understanding of the distribution of the 
cartridge performance for the cartridges examined. 
 
• Mean Thermal Efficiency (%) – The average thermal efficiency of the cartridges. 
 
• Thermal Efficiency of Peak Generating Cartridge (%) – The thermal efficiency of the 
cartridge achieving peak gross power. 
 
The cartridge gross power was measured directly, allowing assessment of the cartridges’ 
generation performance. 
 
Cartridge thermal efficiency was determined using the cartridges’ gross power, dimensions, 
thermal conductivity of the cartridge material, and the arithmetic mean temperature difference 
(AMTD). Due to a lack of individual cartridge inlet and outlet temperature measurements, the 
AMTD for cartridge plates was estimated based on thermocouple readings from a sample of 
hot and cold plates. The absence of meaningful timestamps in the cartridge power data 
hindered direct correlation of efficiency data with AMTD. Consequently, this analysis focused 
on overall mean cartridge thermal efficiency and estimated efficiency at peak power generation 
to assess the impact of hot-side inlet temperatures.   
 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   41 
The mean cartridge thermal efficiency was determined from the mean gross cartridge power 
and the mean AMTD for each day as follows:

6.2 Estimated Annual TEG Impact Methodology 
The second part of the evaluation was an estimated analysis of the annual impact of a 
theoretical commercial-scale TEG system at the Holcim Alpena plant, with its atypical long dry 
kilns and waste heat superheater boilers, compared to common cement plants with more 
typical preheater pre-calciner kilns. The study examined the capture of convective waste heat 
using two proposed tapping points to capture waste heat from the cement kilns: the kiln 
exhaust gas stream (Tap Point 1) and the clinker cooler exhaust gas stream (Tap Point 2). 
 
To account for variations in potential use cases, this report focused on two analyses to assess 
the impact of the TEG system:  
 
• Holcim Alpena Plant: This analysis includes the estimation of annual gross, parasitic, 
and net power and electricity generation at Holcim’s Alpena plant based on waste heat 
available exiting the superheater boiler and the clinker cooler exhaust, calculated using 
annual kiln operational data for 2023. See Figure 1 for proposed tap points. 
 
• Typical Installation at Cement Plants: This analysis includes the estimation of annual 
gross, parasitic, and net power and electricity generation of a typical commercial-scale 
installation at cement plants using waste heat of the preheater exhaust gas and clinker 
cooler exhaust gas. A sensitivity analysis was performed for generated waste heat 
calculated from typical clinker production rates, preheater exhaust gas temperatures, 
and clinker cooler exhaust gas temperatures. See Figure 2 for proposed tap points. 
 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   42 
According to the ATS team, the application of a commercial-scale TEG system would consist of 
a gas-to-working-fluid heat exchanger supplying heat to the TEG system (effectively in place of 
the steam-to-working-fluid heat exchanger—HX-001—of the pilot-scale unit). In practice, this 
would consist of installing a heat exchanger that would directly capture convective waste heat 
from the kilns to convey to the working fluid of the TEG arrays (HX-005). The proposed 
configuration assumes each site would have one central heat exchanger to capture waste heat 
from all kilns. Duct work would be connected at the proposed tap-in points of each kiln, 
combining the flows into one waste heat source. The methodology used to determine the waste 
heat conditions is shown in Appendix Section C.ii. 
 
Additionally, as discussed in Section 2.2, the commercial-scale TEG system is currently under 
development with ATS’ first customer (also slated to be Holcim). As the system was in design 
at the time of this study, assumptions were made regarding the configuration and control 
scheme used to convert the available thermal waste heat into electricity. From discussions with 
the ATS team, the commercial system will include common headers to supply hot working fluid 
and cold-side fluid to the TEG arrays. For this analysis, the headers were assumed to supply 
“strings” of TEG arrays arranged in series. The strings of arrays were assumed to be arranged 
in a parallel configuration. See Figure 19 and Figure 20 in Appendix Section C.iv for diagrams 
of the conceptual configuration. This configuration and control scheme is one of many potential 
solutions and is not meant to represent an “optimized” arrangement, for which a comprehensive 
analysis of flow rates, pressure drops, heat exchanger performance, impacts on parasitic loads, 
etc., would be necessary. This configuration was selected following discussions with the ATS 
team to enable application of the regression model developed from the pilot system. For 
example, this configuration ensures that analysis assumptions, such as hot-side flow rate 
through the TEG array, remain within the boundaries of the conditions observed during the pilot 
system's M&V testing while aiming to maximize the total number of arrays. Further discussion 
can be found in Appendix Section C.iv. 
 
Each array in this configuration is analogous to one pilot unit. The system is considered 
modular in design, where the number of strings of arrays and number of arrays per string can 
be tailored to maximize generation in a specific application, dependent on the waste heat 
characteristics. As such, for this analysis, a theoretical design of a TEG system was 
constructed for each potential application, a demonstration of which can be found in Appendix 
Section C.iv. The conceptual design is a matrix of pilot-scale unit arrays consisting of: 
 
• MDesign = The total number of parallel strings of TEG arrays  
 
• NDesign = The total number of TEG arrays in series per string. Each array is analogous to 
the TEG pilot-scale unit, consisting of 573 active thermoelectric cartridges.  
 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   43 
Figure 7 provides a diagram of the conceptual commercial-scale TEG system and heat balance 
for this analysis, including the heat exchanger to capture waste heat (HX-001), and the matrix 
of arrays (pilot-scale units). With the above considerations, it is important to note that the likely 
positive and negative system performance changes when scaling from pilot to commercial size 
cannot be quantified at this time. Examples include factors such as changes in heat transfer 
mediums, heat exchanger selections, losses between HX-001 and HX-005, etc. 
 
To estimate the annual TEG impact, it was necessary to estimate the net power of each 
individual TEG array within the matrix of the TEG commercial system. The analysis consisted 
of the following key steps:  
 
• Regression Model Development – An analysis of the pilot-scale unit test data to 
construct predictive regression models of gross power generation and parasitic power 
loads.  
 
• Regression Model Application – Using the Holcim Alpena operational data for 2023, a 
heat balance of the heat exchanger/TEG system shown in Figure 7 was used to apply 
routine adjustments to the models and estimate the power generated by each TEG 
array in the commercial-scale system for each hour of a typical year, and thus the 
annual electricity generation potential at the site. Using the Alpena plant production 
profile as a guide, the regression models were then applied to typical kiln operational 
parameters to estimate annual generation potential of a typical commercial-scale TEG 
installation at cement plants, using a sensitivity analysis to understand the potential 
impact of key factors on electricity generation. 
 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   44 
 
Figure 7. Schematic showing the heat and mass balance for a conceptual commercial-scale TEG 
application at a cement plant12 
 
i. Cement Plant Recoverable Waste Heat 
 
The evaluation team used the conceptual configuration described above to estimate the waste 
heat recoverable from the Holcim Alpena cement plant and from common cement plant 
operating conditions.  
 
Based on the annual kiln operation data collected for Holcim Alpena in 2023, the cement plant 
operates at an average rate of production and waste heat temperature (combining the waste 
heat boiler and clinker cooler exhaust of all five kilns) of 193 tons of clinker/hour and 200°C, 
respectively. Using a combination of industry assumptions and mass/energy balance 
calculations, the evaluation team estimates Holcim Alpena produces an average of 11,200 kW 
of waste heat each hour, 9,500 kW of which would be capturable through installation of a gas-
 
12 For simplicity, the Lake Water/Cooling Source is shown for the last string in the configuration. A similar 
configuration for the cooling source is used in all “M” strings for the cold side of the system. 

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   45 
to-oil heat exchanger (0.85 effectiveness). A more detailed summary of the methodology and 
results for varying heat exchanger effectiveness can be found in Appendix Section C.ii.  
 
Similar estimations were made for common cement plant operating parameters, with a 
sensitivity analysis performed to account for three cases: variations in preheater exhaust 
temperature, clinker cooler exhaust temperature, and daily clinker production rate. Daily clinker 
production was found to have the most significant impact on waste heat potential, with cement 
plants producing 2,700–41,000 kW of average waste heat per hour, 2,300–34,800 kW of which 
would be capturable with a typical heat exchanger (0.85 effectiveness). Based on the sensitivity 
tests, available and capturable heat increases as each of the operating parameters (preheater 
exhaust temperature, clinker cooler exhaust temperature, and clinker production) increase. 
Estimated available waste heat at Holcim Alpena (assuming 0.85 effectiveness) is shown in

cement plant operating conditions is provided in Table 8. A more detailed summary of the 
methodology and results can be found in Appendix Section C.ii.

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   46 
 
ii. Regression Model Development 
 
The first step in estimating the annual performance of a theoretical commercial-scale TEG 
system was to develop regression models to predict the performance of each of the pilot-scale 
systems (arrays) the system comprised. Using collected test data, regression models were 
developed for the key performance variables relative to operating parameters to be applied to 
the annual operating profiles of the cement plants. 
 
Gross and Parasitic Power Models 
 
One of the key performance variables for the TEG system is the gross power, or the total power 
generated by the system. Gross power relies on the operating conditions of the hot side and 
cold side of the TEG system. Due to the Seebeck effect, gross power is particularly dependent 
on the temperature difference between the hot and cold sides of the TEG system.  
 
Another key performance variable for the TEG system is the parasitic power, or the total power 
consumed by the supplementary equipment of the TEG system (pumps, controls, and 
programmable logic controller). Once obtained, the parasitic power can be deducted from the 
gross power to determine the net power, or the total usable power that could be delivered to the 
site. Similar to gross power, parasitic power also depends on the operating conditions of the hot 
side and cold side of the TEG system.  
 
To understand the relationships between the key performance variables and independent 
variables, statistical analyses were conducted to assess the significance of each potential 
variable and their combination. A schematic showing these variables as they relate to the TEG 
pilot system is shown in Figure 8. Three variables were found to be of particular significance in 
predicting gross power generated by the system and parasitic power consumed by the system:  
 
• ΔTin-HX005 = The temperature difference (°C) between the working fluid and cold-side fluid 
entering the TEG array. 
 
• 𝑣̇cin-HX005 = The volumetric flow rate (l/m) of the cold-side fluid entering the HX-005 array.  
 
• 𝑣̇hin-HX005 = The volumetric flow rate (l/m) of the hot working fluid entering the HX-005 
array. 
 
  

Thermoelectric Generation System Operating & Performance Technical Report |   47 
 
Figure 8. Simplified schematic of pilot TEG unit 
 
The volumetric flow rate of the hot working fluid entering the array (𝑣̇hin-HX005) and the cooling 
fluid entering the array (𝑣̇cin-HX005) were measured directly in liters per minute by the ATS control 
system during testing. The temperature difference between the hot entering fluid and cold 
entering fluid (ΔTin-HX005) was calculated by:  
 
∆𝑇𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 = 𝑇ℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 − 𝑇𝑐𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 (10) 
 
Where: 
 
Thin-HX005 = the temperature of the hot working fluid at the array inlet (°C)  
 
Tcin-HX005 = the temperature of the cold fluid at the array inlet (°C)  
 
The relationship between the gross and parasitic power and the three independent variables is 
demonstrated visually by the scatter plots presented in Appendix Section C.v. As expected, the 
gross power increases as the temperature difference between the hot and cold side increases, 
in keeping with the Seebeck effect. Similarly, the gross power increases as the working fluid 
and cold-side fluid flow rates increase, since higher flow rates maintain hotter and colder 
average temperatures, respectively, throughout the cartridges in the array. As a result, the 
maximum gross power was observed when the temperature difference, working-fluid flow rate, 
and cold-side fluid flow rate each were maximized. Note however that at higher flow rates, 
parasitic loads also increase (primarily due to high required pumping power), resulting in a 
negative impact on net power, and a potential trade-off when selecting optimum flow rate. 
During the testing period at the Holcim Alpena site, net power was also maximized at maximum 
flow rates. However, since Holcim Alpena was able to operate without cold-side pumps, this 
(and the resulting model) does not reflect potential parasitic load impacts of cold-side pumping

𝑃𝑎𝑟𝑎𝑠𝑖𝑡𝑖𝑐 𝑃𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟 (𝑘𝑊) = ∆𝑇𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ 𝑎2 + 𝑣̇𝑐𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005  ∗ 𝑏2 + 𝑣̇ℎ𝑖𝑛−𝐻𝑋005 ∗ 𝑐2 + 𝑑2 (12) 
 
In the above equations, “a,” “b,” and “c” are the regression coefficients for the independent 
variables ΔTin-HX005, 𝑣̇cin-HX005, and 𝑣̇hin-HX
